hook: You already know how to work with whole numbers like $3$, $47$, and $1{,}200$ — but what happens when amounts fall *between* whole numbers, like the $\$4.79$ price tag on a snack or the $9.8$ seconds of a sprint record? Whole numbers alone can't capture those in-between values precisely. In this unit, you'll unlock the power of decimals and discover how they quietly show up in almost every number you encounter in real life.
concept: Every topic in this unit is about understanding and operating on numbers that extend beyond the ones place — the idea that our base-ten number system stretches infinitely in *both* directions, formally expressed through place value to the right of the decimal point.

topic_roadmap.description: You will learn how each digit to the right of the decimal point represents a specific place value — tenths, hundredths, thousandths — so you can read and write decimals with confidence.
Master comparing decimals like $0.4$ and $0.38$ by aligning place values, so you can correctly order any list of decimal numbers from least to greatest or greatest to least.
Apply rounding rules to simplify decimals to a given place value, a skill you'll use constantly when estimating prices, measurements, and calculator results.
You will learn to add and subtract decimals accurately by lining up decimal points, so calculations like $12.5 + 3.78$ are just as straightforward as adding whole numbers.

Master multiplying decimals by using whole-number multiplication and then placing the decimal point correctly, so you can solve real-world problems like finding the cost of $3.5$ pounds of fruit at $\$2.40$ per pound.
You will learn to divide decimals by whole numbers and by other decimals, giving you the tools to split costs, convert rates, and interpret data with precision.
Apply division and place-value reasoning to switch freely between fractions and decimals — like seeing that $\frac{3}{4} = 0.75$ — so you can choose whichever form makes a problem easier to solve.
